translation: I fear that the enemy will come.
options: fearing clause
purpose clause
result clause
indirect command
explanation: timeō (I fear) is a verb of fearing; nē + subjunctive (veniant) after a fear verb signals a fearing clause. Crucially, nē here means 'that' (positive fear) — the opposite of its meaning in purpose and indirect commands. 'I fear that the enemy will come' = a real fear.

translation: I fear that the storm will not calm down.
options: fearing clause
indirect command
purpose clause
result clause
explanation: vereor (I fear) is a verb of fearing; ut + subjunctive (sēdet) after a fear verb signals negative fear — 'I fear that...not'. This is the counter-intuitive reversal: with fear verbs, ut = that...not (negative fear) while nē = that (positive fear). sēdet is present subjunctive of sedeō used intransitively.

sentence: metuit nē frāter in perīculō esset.
display: metuit nē frāter in perīculō esset.
translation: He feared that his brother was in danger.
options: fearing clause
purpose clause
indirect statement
fearing clause
explanation: metuō (I fear) + nē + subjunctive (esset, imperfect — sequence of tenses after perfect metuō) is a fearing clause expressing positive fear. The thing feared is that the brother was in danger. The imperfect subjunctive esset follows the sequence after the perfect main verb.

translation: I fear that the slaves have escaped.
options: fearing clause
indirect command
result clause
purpose clause
explanation: timeō + nē + perfect subjunctive (effūgerint) is a fearing clause about a past event — fearing something has already happened. effūgerint is perfect subjunctive of effugiō (to escape/flee away). The perfect subjunctive after a present fear verb means the feared event may already have occurred.

translation: They were afraid that the angry king would return.
options: fearing clause
temporal clause
indirect statement
result clause
explanation: verebantur (they were afraid) + nē + subjunctive (reverterētur, imperfect — after imperfect main verb) is a fearing clause. irrātus (angry) is a PPP used as an adjective modifying rēx. The imperfect subjunctive follows sequence of tenses after the imperfect verebantur.

options: I fear that the journey is long.
I fear that the journey is not long.
I order that the journey be long.
I am afraid lest the journey not be long.
correct: I fear that the journey is long.
explanation: timeō + nē + subjunctive (sit) is a fearing clause. nē after a fear verb = 'that' (positive fear). This is the key rule to remember: nē with fear verbs expresses positive fear ('I fear that it IS long'), while nē with motion verbs or verbs of ordering expresses negative purpose or command.

options: We feared that there was not enough water.
We feared that there was enough water.
We ordered that there be enough water.
We hurried so that there would be enough water.
correct: We feared that there was not enough water.
explanation: metuēbāmus (we feared) + ut + subjunctive (esset) is a fearing clause. ut after a fear verb = 'that...not' (negative fear). 'We feared that there was NOT enough water' — the feared thing is the negative outcome (not enough water). satis aquae = 'enough of water' (partitive genitive).

options: We fear that you will be captured.
We fear that you will not be captured.
We order that you not be captured.
We ran so that you would not be captured.
correct: We fear that you will be captured.
explanation: timēmus + nē + subjunctive passive (capiāris, present passive subjunctive of capiō) is a fearing clause. nē = 'that' with fear verbs. capiāris is 2nd person singular present passive subjunctive — 'you be captured'. The fear is that capture happens, not that it doesn't.

options: He was afraid that the leader would be killed.
He was afraid that the leader would not be killed.
He ordered that the leader not be killed.
He was so afraid that the leader was killed.
correct: He was afraid that the leader would be killed.
explanation: verēbātur (he was afraid) + nē + imperfect subjunctive passive (interficerētur) is a fearing clause. nē = positive fear (that it WOULD happen). interficerētur is imperfect passive subjunctive of interficiō, following sequence of tenses after the imperfect verēbātur.

options: I fear that the sailors are not safe.
I fear that the sailors are safe.
I order the sailors to be safe.
I hurry so that the sailors are safe.
correct: I fear that the sailors are not safe.
explanation: timeō + ut + subjunctive (sint) is a fearing clause expressing negative fear — 'I fear that...not'. The feared thing is their NOT being safe. ut with fear verbs signals that the thing feared is the negative/absence of the stated condition. Reverse from normal: here I fear they are NOT salvī.
